BharSt_2.1.en: Salutations to Love, whose ways are beyond description, and who can make even Śiva, Brahmā, and Viṣṇu serve at the household doors of doe-eyed women.
BharSt_2.1.te: వర్ణనకు అందని చమత్కారంతో, శివుడు, బ్రహ్మ, విష్ణువుల్నే హరిణి కన్నుల స్త్రీల ఇంటి వద్ద సేవకుల్లా నిలబెట్టగల మన్మథుడికి నమస్కారం.

BharSt_2.2: With smiles, moods, modesty, fear, sidelong glances, half-averted faces, playful words, jealousy, and mock quarrels, lovers bind the heart in countless ways.
BharSt_2.3: Clever brows, sidelong looks, tender speech, shy laughter, and a gait that is at once hesitant and graceful: these are both the adornment and the weapon of love.
BharSt_2.4: A young woman's changing glances, now shy, now playful, now bold, scatter enchantment in every direction like a sudden flowering of blue lotuses.
BharSt_2.5: Face, eyes, color, hair, breasts, hips, and soft speech all seem naturally adorned in youth. Beauty appears complete not because art forces it, but because it ripens on its own.
BharSt_2.6: A slightly innocent smile, fluid glance, fresh speech, and tender movement: as youth begins to unfold, almost everything in the deer-eyed beloved appears charming.
BharSt_2.7: What is most worth seeing, hearing, tasting, touching, and even meditating upon? For the lover, each answer returns to the beloved and to the play of her new youth.
BharSt_2.8: The jingling of ornaments, the movement of girdles, and startled deer-like glances easily overpower the mind. Youth does not need argument when charm itself is persuasive.
BharSt_2.9: Marked with saffron, trembling at the breast, ringing with anklets, the beloved wins over the world not by force but by presence.
BharSt_2.10: Poets are wrong to call women weak. If even gods are conquered by the restless power of their glance, what kind of weakness is that?
BharSt_2.11: Love itself seems to obey the command of the lovely-browed woman, moving wherever her eyes silently direct it.
BharSt_2.12: Hair, eyes, mouth, teeth, breasts, and the whole body become occasions for attachment. Even what ought to calm the mind can stir longing instead.
BharSt_2.13: What strange archery is this in you, innocent one? You pierce hearts not with arrows but with your very qualities.

BharSt_2.14.en: Even with lamps, fire, stars, the moon, and lotuses all present, the world is darkness to me without the doe-eyed beloved.
BharSt_2.14.te: దీపాలు, అగ్ని, నక్షత్రాలు, చంద్రుడు, కమలాలు అన్నీ ఉన్నా, ఆ హరిణికన్నుల ప్రియురాలు లేకపోతే నాకు ఈ జగత్తంతా చీకటే.

BharSt_2.15: Let the heavy breasts, wavering eyes, trembling brows, and desire-colored lips disturb the heart as they will. Yet somehow the fine line of hair upon the midriff torments even more.
BharSt_2.16: With a moon-bright face, dark-blue hair, and lotus-red hands, she shone as if she were made entirely of jewels.
BharSt_2.17: With the weight of her breasts, the brightness of her moon-like face, and the slow movement of her feet, she seemed like a walking constellation.
BharSt_2.18: If beauty so agitates your mind, do not pretend surprise. Earn the inward worth to meet what you desire; such fulfillment does not come without preparation.
BharSt_2.19: These graceful gestures of doe-eyed women, fragrant with youth and ready for love, become expert thieves of the heart and teachers of new emotional states.
BharSt_2.20: Private speech between lovers, sweet with affection, trust, softness, and desire, steals the mind more deeply than public beauty ever can.
BharSt_2.21: A slender woman wandered from shade to shade among the forest trees, lifting her breast-cloth with one hand to ward off the moon's rays.

BharSt_2.22.en: In absence, one longs only to see; after seeing, one longs for the joy of embrace; once embraced, one desires that there be no separation at all.
BharSt_2.22.te: కనిపించనప్పుడు చూడాలనే కోరిక; చూసిన తర్వాత ఆలింగనం కోసం తపన; ఆలింగనం తర్వాత ఇక విరహమే లేకుండా ఉండాలని ఆశ.

BharSt_2.23: Jasmine in the hair, yawning on the lips, sandal and saffron on the body, and a languid beloved resting on the chest: what remains of heaven beyond this?
BharSt_2.24: Love advances in stages: expectation, modesty, hesitation, surrender, tenderness, confidence, and finally bold intimacy. What appears sudden is often a slow ripening of trust and desire.
BharSt_2.25: Fortunate indeed are those who drink the nectar of their beloved's lips while her hair falls loose, her eyes half-close, and the exertion of love leaves her cheeks moist.
BharSt_2.26: When both lovers half-close their eyes and silently confirm one another's delight, that shared unspoken knowing is itself the fullest working of desire.
BharSt_2.27: It is unseemly that the disturbances of desire remain even in old age, yet human life itself often seems measured only up to the falling of a woman's breasts and the fading of passion.
BharSt_2.28: What use is limitless wealth once youth is already slipping away? Before beauty fades, the lover wants nearness more urgently than possessions.
BharSt_2.29: Youth is the house of passion, the cause of great suffering, the seedbed of delusion, the cloud that hides the moon of wisdom, and the chief ally of desire. Nothing else gathers so many dangers into one place.
BharSt_2.30: When fresh youth arrives like rain over the tree of desire, only a rare person remains inwardly steady. Most hearts are moved by its force.
BharSt_2.31: How could a pure mind remain steady in this unsubstantial world of degrading service to bad rulers, if lotus-eyed young women with swinging girdles and full breasts did not exist to captivate it?
BharSt_2.32: Who would ever bow his proud head in humiliation before others if women with fawn-like eyes did not become the arrows of love, even when a nobler refuge in the Himalayan caves lies open?
BharSt_2.33: The end of worldly life would not be far at all, if only the difficult crossing made of wine-dark eyes were not in the middle of the path.
BharSt_2.34: Give the forest deer the bamboo shoots, and the brides the red betel leaves split by polished nails. Each world has its proper delight, and desire chooses accordingly.
BharSt_2.35: Even a single extra verse from a beloved can feel weightier than formal learning, because affection changes the measure by which words are received.
BharSt_2.36: Let the noble speak plainly, after envy is set aside: which is more worthy of service, the rounded slopes of mountains or the rounded hips of smiling women?
BharSt_2.37: In this world as unstable as a dream, the learned have only two paths: let time pass in the boat of truth-knowledge, or else in the close embrace of lovely women.
BharSt_2.38: Make your home either in the Ganges, destroyer of sin, or in the twin breasts of a young woman, destroyers of composure.
BharSt_2.39: Why all these clever but empty arguments? For men there are really only two things worth serving: the fresh youth of beautiful women, or the forest.

BharSt_2.40.en: I speak the truth without partiality: nothing is more delightful than the beloved, and nothing causes sorrow more deeply either.
BharSt_2.40.te: పక్షపాతం లేకుండా నిజమే చెబుతున్నాను: ప్రియురాలికన్నా మధురమైనదేమీ లేదు; ప్రియురాలివల్ల కలిగే దుఃఖంకన్నా లోతైనదీ లేదు.

BharSt_2.41: Calling her lotus-eyed, broad-hipped, full-breasted, moon-faced, and lovely-browed, even a wise man delights in praise. Such is the outrageous mischief of delusion before a body that is, in truth, impure.
BharSt_2.42: Remembered, the beloved causes heat; seen, she causes madness; touched, she causes delusion. How then is she called 'beloved'?
BharSt_2.43: She is pure nectar only while she remains before the eyes. Once she passes out of sight, she can exceed even poison in the grief she leaves behind.
BharSt_2.44: Other than the woman of shapely hips, there is neither nectar nor poison. Attached, she is a vine of ambrosia; detached, a creeper of venom.
BharSt_2.45: Woman is a whirlpool of doubts, a city of boldness, a storehouse of faults, a field of deception, a barrier at heaven's gate, and an entrance to hell. What astonishing snare, at once poisonous and sweet, was fashioned for living beings.
BharSt_2.46: Truly the moon is not her face, nor blue lotuses her eyes, nor gold her body. Yet even knowing this, the dull mind remains deceived by poetic comparisons and still serves the fleshly form of the doe-eyed woman.
BharSt_2.47: Love delights in small signs, but the same signs can also become causes of unrest. What charms the heart can unsettle it just as easily.
BharSt_2.48: That lotus-face of the slender beloved, home of lip-nectar, now seems surpassingly sweet like ripe fruit. But once the season passes, the same charm can turn harsh and joyless, almost like poison.
BharSt_2.49: If you do not wish to drown in the ocean of worldly life, stay away from the river in the shape of a beloved: its triple folds are waves, its full breasts are waterfowl, and its lotus-face shines invitingly at the surface.
BharSt_2.50: Words may be given to one person, glances to another, and thoughts to someone else entirely. Desire does not always move with the same fidelity it claims.
BharSt_2.51: There is honey in the speech of women, but in the heart there may be only poison. That is why one kisses the lip yet must be ready for the blow that follows.
BharSt_2.52: Friend, keep far from the fire-poison of the sidelong glance, from the naturally dangerous serpent that is woman with her hood of playful charm. A common snake can be cured with herbs; not so the bite of seductive beauty.
BharSt_2.53: In the ocean of becoming, Love the fisherman casts a hook called woman, baited with her lips. Mortal fish, greedy for that morsel, are quickly pulled out and cooked in the fire of attachment.
BharSt_2.54: Mind, do not travel through the wilderness of the female body, hard to cross because of the mountain-forts of the breasts. There waits the bandit called Love.
BharSt_2.55: Those who move inside desire without reflection think its sweetness is stable. But delight shifts quickly, and so the heart must learn to see through its own excitement.
BharSt_2.56: Sweet song, dance, fragrance, and the touch of breasts: so the senses, having ruined my concern for what is highest, have cheated me like five skilled tricksters.
BharSt_2.57: This fever of love cannot be reached by mantra, medicine, or even hundreds of rites of pacification. It twists the limbs, confuses the sight, and spins the mind like an epileptic seizure of desire.
BharSt_2.58: Who, with even a trace of judgment, could delight in courtesans who sell their beauty to the blind, the ugly, the diseased, the old, the coarse, and the base alike?
BharSt_2.59: A courtesan is a flame of desire, fed by the fuel of beauty, into which lovers pour both their youth and their wealth as offerings.
BharSt_2.60: Even what appears attractive is not fit for every kind of love. Desire loses its dignity when it ignores context, history, and self-respect.
BharSt_2.61: Blessed indeed are those whose minds do not distort when they behold broad-eyed young women with proud full breasts and the vine of triple folds shining over a slender waist.
BharSt_2.62: Young girl, why keep casting these slow budding glances? Stop, stop, your effort is wasted. Our childhood has passed, we dwell now at the forest's edge, and we see the whole net of the world as straw.
BharSt_2.63: This young woman keeps throwing her blue-lotus glance at me, but what does she want? My delusion is gone, the fever born of Love's arrows is cooled, yet the poor girl does not stop.
BharSt_2.64: Love, why rattle your bowstring? Cuckoo, why waste your sweet call? Gentle one, enough of your soft and clever sidelong glances. My mind now dwells in the nectar of meditation on the moon-crested Lord.

BharSt_2.65.en: For those whose minds remain joined, there is union even in separation. But when hearts have truly come apart, even physical nearness becomes another form of distance.
BharSt_2.65.te: మనసులు కలిసే ఉంటే విరహంలోనూ సంగమమే ఉంటుంది. హృదయాలు నిజంగా దూరమైతే, శారీరక సమీపమూ మరో రకమైన దూరంగానే మారుతుంది.

BharSt_2.66: If she no longer lives, what is the use of going? And if she still lives, what difference does it make? Thinking thus as the first monsoon clouds gather, the traveler does not return home.
BharSt_2.67: Wise people should withdraw from fleeting delight in erotic attachment and instead join themselves to compassion, friendship, and understanding. Ornamented bodies do not protect anyone from suffering.
BharSt_2.68: When deep friendship with the Self ripens through practice of yoga, what need remains for the speech, lips, moon-faces, sighs, embraces, and love-play of beloved women?
BharSt_2.69: When ignorance born of love's darkness ruled me, the whole world seemed made of women. Now that sharp discernment has anointed my sight, even the three worlds appear as Brahman.
BharSt_2.70: Even the lamp of clear discernment burns only so long as it is not struck by the restless edge of the beloved's glance.
BharSt_2.71: The learned can talk as much as they like about renouncing attachment, but who is truly able to abandon the red-jeweled girdle around the hips of lotus-eyed women?
BharSt_2.72: The false wise man who condemns women only cheats himself and others, for even the fruit of asceticism is heaven, and heaven too is filled with heavenly women.
BharSt_2.73: What the mind pursues under the spell of attraction often says more about its hunger than about the person before it.
BharSt_2.74: A man remains on the right path, master of his senses, modest and disciplined, only until the blue-lashed arrows of playful women's glances strike and rob the heart of firmness.
BharSt_2.75: When women begin something in the frenzy of passionate love, even Brahmā himself would hesitate to stand in the way.
BharSt_2.76: Greatness, learning, good birth, and discrimination all last only until the fire kindled by Love's five arrows starts burning in the limbs.
BharSt_2.77: Even the learned and well-born rarely reach the good path in this world, because the crooked eyebrow of a lovely woman opens the gate of hell like a key.
BharSt_2.78: Even a miserable dog still runs after a female dog. Love's force can survive ridicule, decay, and exhaustion; desire does not always obey good judgment.
BharSt_2.79: Fools abandon the sovereign seal of woman, conqueror of Love himself and giver of every delight, and go chasing false fruits. Love strips them bare and drives them into strange ascetic costumes and extremes.
BharSt_2.80: If even Viśvāmitra and Parāśara, living on air, water, and leaves, were deluded at the sight of a beautiful woman's face, then if ordinary people who eat rich food can master the senses, the Vindhya mountains might as well float on the sea.
BharSt_2.81: Fragrant winds, new shoots on the branches, the yearning call of cuckoos, and the shining faces of women damp with light sweat: when spring spreads across the earth, whose qualities do not awaken?

BharSt_2.82.en: The cuckoo's sweet call and the Malaya breeze are delightful, yet for one in separation they become torment. In misfortune, even nectar can taste like poison.
BharSt_2.82.te: కోకిల మధుర గానం, మలయ గాలి ఆనందమే; కానీ విరహంలో ఉన్నవానికి అవే యాతనగా మారుతాయి. దుఃఖకాలంలో అమృతమూ విషంలా అనిపిస్తుంది.

BharSt_2.83: A private place beside the beloved, a bower smiling with vines, the calls of cuckoos at the ear, a gathering with a few good poets, and moonlight: such varied spring nights bring delight to certain fortunate hearts.
BharSt_2.84: Union is precious because it is fragile. Love becomes clearer when one remembers that delight cannot be forced to remain.
BharSt_2.85: Let offended pride hold its place in the hearts of lovers only until the sandal-scented Malaya breeze begins to blow.
BharSt_2.86: When spring fills the directions with the heavy fragrance of mango blossoms and maddens the bees with sweetness, who does not feel longing awaken?
BharSt_2.87: Cool sandal paste, doe-eyed women, water-houses, flowers, moonlight, gentle breezes, and clean palace terraces all increase both intoxication and desire in summer.
BharSt_2.88: Garlands, breeze, moonlight, fragrance, wine, fine clothing, and the company of lotus-eyed women make the heat of summer bearable for the fortunate.
BharSt_2.89: Moonlight, sandal fragrance, the moon itself, and the lotus-face of the beloved all stir the inner being of the passionate, but not of one who has turned away from sensual contact.
BharSt_2.90: The rainy season, with women dressed to kindle passion, the scent of blooming jasmine, and the fullness of young breasts, brings delight to almost every heart.
BharSt_2.91: Season, mood, gesture, and memory all intensify longing. The outer world becomes part of the inner weather of desire.
BharSt_2.92: Clouds mass above, mountains ring with dancing peacocks, and the earth is whitened by shoots and blossoms. Where can the traveler turn his eyes in such a season?
BharSt_2.93: Here lightning flashes, there ketaki gives its scent, there thunder roars, and there peacocks cry. How will the days of separation pass for lotus-eyed lovers in such a ripened monsoon?
BharSt_2.94: In darkness pierced by lightning, with heavy clouds rumbling and rain falling in sheets, that golden flash spreads both delight and desolation through the hearts of free-moving beauties on the road.
BharSt_2.95: When rain keeps lovers indoors, the cold gives reason for close embrace, and cool breezes relieve exhaustion, then for the fortunate even a gloomy day becomes a blessed one.
BharSt_2.96: After love's exertion, under moonlight and wine, the fortunate drink autumn water beside an exhausted beloved. Pleasure here is luxurious, intimate, and fleeting.
BharSt_2.97: In winter, food, warmth, fragrance, bright garments, and close embraces within the house become the whole landscape of pleasure for the fortunate.
BharSt_2.98: For those without a warm deer-eyed beloved close at the throat, winter nights lengthen into something like the house of death itself.
BharSt_2.99: Cold-season winds act almost like expert lovers themselves: kissing cheeks, loosening garments, raising gooseflesh, and stirring the whole body into response.
BharSt_2.100: In the turbulence of passion, every touch, glance, and pause acquires force. Such intensity is real, but it should not be mistaken for permanence.
